# The Context Window as a Unified Optimization Surface: Why Memory, Tools, Instructions, and Plans Must Be Co-Engineered

The dominant approach to building LLM-based agents treats memory management, tool integration, instruction design, and planning as separable engineering concerns—each addressed by its own subsystem, its own literature, and its own optimization target. Memory systems decide what to store and retrieve. Tool frameworks standardize invocation and discovery. Prompt engineering refines instructions. Planning modules decompose tasks into subtasks. Yet at every inference step, all of these subsystems converge on a single shared resource: the finite context window that conditions the model's next generation. The connecting insight that none of these individual research threads fully articulates is that the context window is not merely a container into which these components deposit their outputs—it is an optimization surface where the joint configuration of memory, tool outputs, instructions, and plan state determines agent performance, and where optimizing any one dimension in isolation leaves most of the available performance on the table.

### The Convergence Bottleneck

Consider what actually occupies an agent's context at a given reasoning step in a multi-step workflow. The system prompt encodes role identity, behavioral constraints, and tool descriptions—which in protocol-heavy configurations like MCP can consume 40% or more of available tokens before any task-specific content appears. The plan representation specifies the current subtask, its dependencies, and its relationship to the overall goal. Retrieved memories—whether episodic traces of past executions, semantic knowledge from policy stores, or procedural templates of known-good action sequences—occupy further space. Tool outputs from prior steps, potentially verbose JSON payloads or lengthy knowledge base excerpts, fill additional capacity. And the agent's own prior reasoning traces—chain-of-thought steps, self-corrections, intermediate conclusions—accumulate across the execution history.

Each of these components has its own management system. Memory architectures implement tiered storage with retention policies. Tool protocols handle discovery and invocation. Prompt optimization techniques refine instruction phrasing. Planning frameworks structure task decomposition. But none of these systems is aware of the others' contributions to the shared context. A memory retrieval system that surfaces five relevant episodic traces does not know that tool descriptions have already consumed a third of the window. A tool output that returns a complete JSON response does not know that the agent's plan only requires a single field from that response three steps from now. An instruction set that was carefully optimized at deployment time does not know that accumulated episodic context has introduced information that contradicts or supersedes its original directives.

This mutual unawareness explains a puzzling empirical pattern: aggregate performance is relatively insensitive to memory management style (complete versus summarized), yet complex workflows exhibit catastrophically low reliability. The insensitivity reflects the fact that both complete and summarized memory are comparably suboptimal—neither is conditioned on the joint state of all other context components. The low reliability reflects the compounding cost of this suboptimality across many steps, where each step's context is assembled from independently managed components that collectively produce a prompt that is noisy, diluted, or internally inconsistent.

### Plan-Conditioned Joint Optimization

The key mechanism that transforms context management from a set of independent subsystem decisions into a unified optimization problem is conditioning on plan state. An agent's plan—represented as a DAG of subtasks with dependencies, preconditions, and anticipated information flows—provides the structure needed to determine, at each step, what information is relevant not just now but across the upcoming execution horizon.

Plan conditioning changes the optimization target for every context component simultaneously. For memory retrieval, it shifts the relevance criterion from similarity to the current query toward anticipated utility across the next k subtasks—preserving an episodic trace not because it matches the current step but because it contains evidence that will be synthesized three steps downstream in a multi-hop reasoning chain. For tool output processing, it enables aggressive filtering at the point of ingestion: if the plan indicates that only a transaction ID from a verbose API response will be needed in a subsequent verification step, the rest of the payload can be discarded before it ever enters working memory. For instructions, plan conditioning supports active rewriting that maintains coherence between the agent's behavioral directives and the evolving task state—preventing the instruction drift that occurs when accumulated context gradually buries or contradicts the original operating constraints. For the plan representation itself, plan conditioning enables progressive compression: completed subtasks can be collapsed into summary representations while upcoming subtasks retain full structural detail.

This joint optimization is qualitatively different from optimizing each component independently. Independent optimization of memory retrieval might surface the most semantically similar past experiences, but those experiences might be redundant with information already present in tool outputs. Independent compression of tool outputs might preserve the most salient fields, but salience without plan awareness is evaluated against the current step rather than the execution horizon. Independent instruction maintenance might preserve the original system prompt verbatim, but verbatim preservation wastes tokens on directives that are no longer applicable given completed subtasks. Only when all components are optimized jointly against the plan structure can the context window achieve the information density and coherence that complex workflows demand.

### Role-Aware Context Distribution in Multi-Agent Systems

The joint optimization problem becomes more complex—and more consequential—in multi-agent architectures where context must be distributed across agents with different roles. An orchestrator agent needs full-task memories, plan-level state, and high-level summaries of tool interactions to support decomposition and delegation. A task agent needs subtask-specific memories, detailed tool output from its assigned operations, and narrowly scoped instructions relevant to its current function. The same underlying information must be transformed differently depending on which agent will consume it.

Current approaches to role-aware memory retrieval address this partially: orchestrators receive full-task memories while task agents receive subtask memories. But this role-awareness operates only on the memory dimension. Tool outputs are not role-filtered—a task agent receives the same verbose API response regardless of whether it needs the full payload or a single extracted value. Instructions are not role-compressed—a task agent may receive system-level directives about orchestration behavior that are irrelevant to its execution role. Plan representations are not role-scoped—a task agent may see the full task DAG when it only needs its local subtask and immediate dependencies.

A unified context policy would apply role-aware optimization across all context components simultaneously, producing a prompt for each agent that contains exactly the memory, tool output, instructions, and plan information relevant to that agent's current function—and nothing more. The empirical finding that procedural memory can enable smaller models to approach the performance of larger models suggests that context quality, not context quantity, is the binding constraint. Smaller models with precisely curated context may outperform larger models drowning in role-irrelevant information, which reframes the model-to-role assignment problem: the optimal model for a given role depends not just on the role's reasoning demands but on the quality of context engineering applied to that role's prompt.

### The Temporal Dimension of Relevance

A critical aspect that emerges from treating context as a unified optimization surface is the temporal structure of information relevance. In a multi-step workflow, information has a relevance lifecycle: it may be irrelevant when first acquired, critical several steps later during synthesis, and obsolete after the synthesis step completes. Tool outputs, episodic memories, and intermediate reasoning traces all exhibit this temporal relevance pattern, but current systems manage relevance using only instantaneous criteria—recency, similarity to the current query, or fixed retention windows.

Plan-aware context engineering introduces a temporal relevance model where each piece of information in context is annotated (implicitly or explicitly) with its anticipated consumption point in the plan DAG. Information whose consumption point is the current step receives high priority. Information whose consumption point is several steps ahead is retained but may be compressed. Information whose consumption point has passed is aggressively pruned or collapsed into a summary. This temporal model interacts with task structure: workflows with local dependencies (where each step consumes only the immediately preceding output) permit aggressive pruning and short lookahead horizons, while multi-hop reasoning chains (where evidence acquired early is synthesized much later) require longer retention and more conservative compression.

The temporal dimension also explains why the binary choice between complete and summarized memory is a false dichotomy. The optimal strategy is neither to retain everything nor to summarize everything but to maintain a temporally graded representation where information at different points in its relevance lifecycle receives different levels of detail. Recently acquired, soon-to-be-consumed information is retained at full fidelity. Information in the middle of its lifecycle is compressed to its plan-relevant essence. Information past its consumption point is either discarded or distilled into a minimal summary that preserves only what might be needed for error recovery or replanning.

### Context Engineering as the Integration Layer

The unifying claim is that context engineering—the discipline of continuously optimizing the content, structure, and allocation of what occupies agent attention—is not one more subsystem alongside memory, tools, planning, and instructions. It is the integration layer that governs how all of these subsystems contribute to the shared resource of the context window. Memory architectures define what information is available; context engineering determines what subset is active. Tool protocols define how external services are invoked; context engineering determines how their outputs are transformed before entering the reasoning loop. Planning frameworks define task structure; context engineering uses that structure to condition every other optimization. Instruction design defines behavioral constraints; context engineering maintains those constraints as living directives that evolve with the task state.

The practical consequence is that advances in any individual subsystem—better memory retrieval, more efficient tool protocols, more sophisticated planning algorithms—will yield diminishing returns unless they are coordinated through a context-level optimization that accounts for their joint contribution to the prompt. The agent systems that achieve reliable performance on complex, procedurally rigid workflows will be those that treat the context window not as a passive receptacle but as the primary site of engineering effort, where memory, tools, plans, and instructions are continuously co-optimized against the agent's evolving information needs.

## See Also

- [[z20260318-060|Context Engineering as the Unifying Discipline for Agent Memory, Compression, and Tool Integration]] — elaborates the concept of context engineering as a unifying discipline and develops the plan-aware compression and instruction coherence mechanisms that this note identifies as components of joint context optimization
- [[z20260318-001|Multi-Agent Architectures for Workflow Automation]] — grounds the role-aware memory retrieval and procedural memory mechanisms that context-level optimization must coordinate across agent boundaries in multi-agent systems
- [[z20260318-030|Cognitive Memory Architectures for AI Agents]] — provides the tripartite memory model (working, episodic, semantic) whose outputs converge at the context window bottleneck identified here as the unified optimization surface
- [[z20260318-020|Memory and Context Management in LLM-Based Agents]] — supplies the empirical findings on memory style insensitivity and enterprise reliability gaps that motivate the shift from independent subsystem optimization to joint context-level optimization
- [[z20260318-032|Tool Use, Action Interfaces, and Integration Challenges in LLM Agent Systems]] — details the tool output verbosity, protocol prompt bloat, and integration fragmentation that constitute major sources of context pollution requiring plan-conditioned filtering
